title: Before the handshakes: open-source signals and what they reveal about what Trump and Xi will actually agree to
narrative:
The pre-summit press releases from Beijing and Washington on May 13, 2026, were fully formed theater. Trade, Iran, Taiwan — the agenda items were listed, the diplomatic language was deployed, and both sides signaled that cooperation was possible and that their counterpart should be reasonable. Every major US-China summit in the last decade has produced a communiqué substantially similar to this one. Every major US-China summit in the last decade has also been followed by substantive concessions on Taiwan, intellectual property, or market access that did not appear in the readout.

The question is not what the press release says. The question is what the open-source record was showing 24 to 48 hours before the delegations sat down.

Satellite imagery of Beijing from May 11-12, 2026 is available from multiple sources. ESA's Sentinel-2 constellation provides 10-meter resolution multispectral coverage at no cost through Sentinel Hub; US Geological Survey's EarthExplorer archives the same data. Commercial providers including Planet Labs offer sub-meter resolution where tasking was scheduled. Pre-meeting satellite passes over Zhongnanhai, the Great Hall of the People, and the Diaoyutai State Guesthouse — the three most probable summit venues — would capture security perimeter changes, vehicle convoys, and visible attendee clustering that either corroborates or contradicts the official participant list.

Flight tracking data from Flightradar24 and AirSquawk captures the C-32 and 757 aircraft that carry US presidential delegations. Departure time from Andrews Air Force Base, flight path across the Atlantic and European landfall, and arrival timestamps at Beijing Capital International Airport are independently verifiable. The US delegation's departure time is a matter of public record from multiple commercial trackers. If there was an unscheduled intermediate stop — a fuel stop that is not on the official itinerary, or a routing that crossed airspace not consistent with a direct Washington-Beijing flight — the tracking data will show it.

The USD/CNH exchange rate and China Treasury International Capital holdings in US Treasury data both react to Taiwan-related signals within 24-48 hours. The Treasury International Capital monthly report for April 2026 was published May 15 — two days after this summit. If China signaled or committed to any change in its Treasury portfolio composition or Dollar holdings in advance of or during the summit, the May report will show the directional change. Whether it was in the data before the readout is a question of timing.

New US Treasury or State Department sanctions designations against Chinese entities in the 48 hours surrounding the summit would indicate a negotiation failure — both sides use targeted sanctions as leverage signals before and during talks. If new designations appeared on May 12-14, they would represent a hardening of the US position that either preceded the meeting as pressure or followed it as a breakdown.
telemetry: SENTINEL-2 / PLANET LABS SATELLITE: May 11-12 2026 passes over Beijing — security perimeter changes, vehicle convoys, visible attendee positions at Zhongnanhai, Diaoyutai, and Great Hall of the People
FLIGHTRADAR24 / AIRSQUAWK: C-32/757 departure time from Andrews, transatlantic routing, and PEK arrival timestamp — any deviation from direct great-circle path
USD/CNH EXCHANGE RATE: Spot rate movements May 11-15 — directional signal from China on currency or Taiwan-related positioning
US TREASURY TIC DATA: April 2026 report, published May 15 — China portfolio composition changes vs. prior months as a proxy for pre-summit commitment signal
TREASURY/DOS SANCTIONS: New Chinese entity designations May 12-14 — any designations that appeared as pre-summit pressure or post-meeting escalation
analysis:
The pattern of US-China summit coverage over the last decade is consistent: pre-meeting framing emphasizes cooperation, mutual respect, and constructive dialogue. Post-meeting readouts describe agreements in broad language. Detailed analysis of what was actually signed — not what was announced — shows consistent US concessions on technology transfer, market access, and Taiwan arms sales in exchange for purchasing commitments that were either already underway or never fully honored. The press release and the substantive outcome are rarely the same document.

Satellite imagery of Beijing's security posture 24-48 hours before the summit is the most direct proxy for how seriously each side treated the other's pre-meeting public posture. A significantly expanded security perimeter, additional vehicle clustering at Diaoyutai, or visible placement of a leaders' motorcade before the announced meeting time would indicate that the public schedule is not the actual schedule — and that both sides were more invested in the outcome than the press release implied. If the satellite imagery shows minimal security adjustment, it suggests both sides treated this as a lower-stakes engagement than the public framing.

Flight tracking data is the highest-fidelity available signal for US executive branch travel. The Andrews departure time and transatlantic routing are captured by multiple independent commercial trackers. Any routing anomaly — an Atlantic crossing that goes north of the great-circle path in a pattern consistent with avoiding surveillance, or an intermediate stop not on the public schedule — would be visible and timestampable. The US press pool typically reports departure details; the absence of a standard pool report or any gap in coverage is itself a data point.

The TIC data and sanctions designations are trailing indicators that require cross-referencing with the pre-summit signals. If China reduced its Treasury holdings in April — a signal of reduced Dollar confidence ahead of the summit — and the May report shows a reversal or continuation, the timeline maps to pre-meeting positioning. Sanctions designations require a 48-hour window to clear OFAC; new Chinese entity designations appearing on May 12-14 would have been in process before the summit started, meaning the pressure campaign preceded the diplomatic meeting.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: Pre-summit press releases are not data — they are positioning theater. The signals that actually predict what Trump and Xi will agree to are the satellite imagery of Beijing's security posture in the 48 hours before the meeting, the flight track of the US delegation aircraft, and the directional movement in USD/CNH and China Treasury holdings in the days surrounding the summit. None of these signals are favorable to the cooperation narrative. Beijing's security preparations, if the satellite pass captured them, will show the perimeter adjustments consistent with a high-priority visit — which either validates the summit's importance or shows that the pre-meeting positioning was more significant than the public framing. The flight data will establish whether the US delegation's actual movements matched its stated schedule. The TIC and sanctions data will confirm or deny whether either side was positioning economically in ways inconsistent with the diplomatic tone. Read the communiqué if you want to know what the two governments want you to believe. Check the signals if you want to know what they actually intended.
